evals.prompt: I just finished implementing a new feature for our Python FastAPI project. It adds a user notification preferences endpoint. The stop hook passed on the last turn. Can you run the full quality checks to make sure everything is production-ready?
evals.expected_output: A systematic quality check process that verifies stop hook results, runs integration tests with .env sourcing, runs bandit security scanning, and requests user validation before declaring production-ready.
expectations: Checks stop hook output first before proceeding to manual checks (Phase 1)
Runs integration tests using the correct command: set -a && source .env && set +a && PYTHONPATH=. uv run pytest -m integration -v
Runs bandit security scanning using: uv run bandit -c pyproject.toml -r .
Uses TodoWrite to track progress through the quality check phases
Treats every test failure as a blocker — does not skip or dismiss any failures
Asks the user to validate and test the application after automated checks pass (Phase 4)
Only declares production-ready after all four phases complete (stop hook, integration tests, security scan, user validation)

evals.prompt: I ran quality checks and bandit found a B602 shell injection issue in my deploy_utils.py where I'm using subprocess.call with shell=True. I fixed it by switching to subprocess.call with a list. The integration tests passed before the fix. What should I do now?
evals.expected_output: Re-run the full quality pipeline starting from integration tests, then re-run bandit to confirm the fix, then proceed to user validation. Security fixes require re-testing because they can change behavior.
expectations: Instructs to re-run integration tests after the security fix (not just re-run bandit)
Explains WHY re-running integration tests is necessary — security fixes can change behavior, modify validation logic, or alter error handling
Re-runs bandit to confirm the B602 issue is resolved
Does not skip straight to user validation after fixing the security issue
Follows the correct post-security-fix flow: fix -> re-run integration tests -> re-run bandit -> user validation
Treats the re-run as mandatory, not optional

evals.prompt: I'm running quality checks and 3 integration tests are failing. Two are database connection errors and one is a timeout. I didn't change any database code — these failures seem pre-existing. Can I just skip them and move on to the security scan?
evals.expected_output: Firm refusal to skip failing tests. Every failure is a blocker regardless of when it was introduced. Provides systematic debugging guidance for the specific failure types (database connection, timeout).
expectations: Refuses to skip or dismiss the failing integration tests — treats every failure as a blocker
Explicitly states there is no concept of 'pre-existing failures' in quality checks
Provides specific troubleshooting for database connection errors (check .env, verify Docker running, check connection string)
Provides specific troubleshooting for timeout errors (container startup, missing test timeout, slow queries)
Recommends running a single failing test in isolation to diagnose: pytest -k test_name -v -m integration
Does not proceed to security scanning until all integration tests pass
If genuinely unable to fix, instructs to stop and report to user with full details rather than silently skipping
